reference: GATE CS — Probability and Statistics / Probability

map.condition: Probability of A given B is already known
Need to reverse conditioning (know P(B|A), want P(A|B))

formulas.name: Definition of Conditional Probability
formulas.plain: P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B), provided P(B) > 0
formulas.whenToUse: When computing the probability of A given that B has already occurred.

terms.means: probability both A and B occur
probability of the conditioning event B

formulas.trap: Confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A) — they are generally not equal.

formulas.name: Multiplication Rule
formulas.latex: P(A \cap B) = P(A|B) \cdot P(B) = P(B|A) \cdot P(A)
formulas.plain: P(A and B) = P(A|B) * P(B) = P(B|A) * P(A)
formulas.whenToUse: When computing the joint probability of two events using conditional probabilities.

terms.means: conditional probability of A given B
conditional probability of B given A

formulas.trap: The rule extends to chains: P(A∩B∩C) = P(A)·P(B|A)·P(C|A∩B). Students often forget to condition correctly in the chain.

formulas.name: Chain Rule (General Multiplication Rule)
formulas.latex: P\left(\bigcap_{i=1}^{n} A_i\right) = P(A_1)\cdot P(A_2|A_1)\cdot P(A_3|A_1 \cap A_2)\cdots P\left(A_n\,\Big|\,\bigcap_{i=1}^{n-1}A_i\right)
formulas.plain: P(A1 and A2 and ... and An) = P(A1) * P(A2|A1) * P(A3|A1,A2) * ... * P(An|A1,...,A(n-1))
formulas.whenToUse: When computing joint probability of n events by decomposing step by step.
formulas.trap: The order of conditioning matters; applying chain rule with incorrect ordering leads to wrong expressions.

formulas.name: Independent Events
formulas.latex: A \perp B \iff P(A \cap B) = P(A)\cdot P(B) \iff P(A|B) = P(A)
formulas.plain: A and B are independent iff P(A and B) = P(A)*P(B), equivalently P(A|B) = P(A)
formulas.whenToUse: When events do not influence each other; used to simplify joint probabilities.
formulas.trap: Independent events are NOT mutually exclusive (unless one has probability 0). Students often conflate these two concepts.

formulas.name: Mutual Independence (n events)
formulas.latex: P\left(\bigcap_{i \in S} A_i\right) = \prod_{i \in S} P(A_i) \quad \forall\, S \subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}
formulas.plain: All subsets must satisfy: P(intersection of A_i for i in S) = product of P(A_i) for i in S
formulas.whenToUse: When verifying or applying mutual independence of a set of events (stronger than pairwise independence).

terms.means: any non-empty subset of event indices

formulas.trap: Pairwise independence does NOT imply mutual independence. GATE frequently tests this distinction.

formulas.name: Mutually Exclusive vs Independent
formulas.plain: Mutually exclusive: P(A and B) = 0. Independent: P(A and B) = P(A)*P(B). They coincide only if P(A) or P(B) = 0.
formulas.whenToUse: When distinguishing between events that cannot co-occur vs. events that do not influence each other.
formulas.trap: If A and B are mutually exclusive with P(A)>0 and P(B)>0, they are DEPENDENT (knowing one occurred rules out the other).

formulas.name: Law of Total Probability
formulas.plain: P(B) = sum over i of P(B|A_i) * P(A_i), where A_1,...,A_n is a partition of the sample space
formulas.whenToUse: When computing an unconditional probability by conditioning on a partition of the sample space.

terms.means: mutually exclusive and exhaustive partition of sample space
likelihood of B given hypothesis A_i

formulas.trap: The A_i must form a true partition (mutually exclusive AND exhaustive); missing a case or double-counting leads to errors.

formulas.name: Two-Case Total Probability
formulas.whenToUse: When the partition is simply an event A and its complement.
formulas.trap: Forgetting the complement term P(B|A^c)*P(A^c) and only summing over one branch.

formulas.name: Bayes' Theorem
formulas.whenToUse: When updating the probability of hypothesis A_i after observing evidence B (inverse probability).

terms.means: likelihood of evidence B under hypothesis A_i
posterior probability of A_i given evidence B

formulas.trap: Mixing up prior and posterior; the denominator is always P(B), not 1.

formulas.name: Bayes' Theorem (Two Hypotheses)
formulas.whenToUse: When there are exactly two complementary hypotheses (e.g., disease / no disease, defective / non-defective).
formulas.trap: Ignoring the base rate leads to base rate neglect — a common GATE MCQ trap.

formulas.name: Posterior Odds Form of Bayes
formulas.plain: Posterior odds = Likelihood ratio * Prior odds
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing two hypotheses using odds rather than probabilities — simplifies calculations.
formulas.trap: Odds and probability are different; odds of 1:1 means probability 0.5, not 1.

formulas.name: Conditional Independence
formulas.latex: A \perp B \mid C \iff P(A \cap B \mid C) = P(A|C)\cdot P(B|C)
formulas.plain: A and B are conditionally independent given C iff P(A and B | C) = P(A|C)*P(B|C)
formulas.whenToUse: When two events are independent only in the context of a third event (e.g., naive Bayes classifier assumption).

terms.means: A is conditionally independent of B given C

formulas.trap: Conditional independence does NOT imply marginal independence, and vice versa.

formulas.name: Addition Rule (Union)
formulas.latex: P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)
formulas.plain: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)
formulas.whenToUse: When computing the probability of at least one of two events occurring.

terms.means: probability of A or B or both

formulas.trap: Forgetting to subtract P(A∩B) causes double-counting. If A and B are mutually exclusive, P(A∩B)=0.

formulas.name: Complement Rule
formulas.whenToUse: When it is easier to compute the probability of the complement and subtract from 1.
formulas.trap: Applying complement to a conditional: P(A^c|B) = 1 - P(A|B), but P(A|B^c) ≠ 1 - P(A|B).

formulas.name: Inclusion-Exclusion (Three Events)
formulas.latex: P(A \cup B \cup C) = P(A)+P(B)+P(C)-P(A\cap B)-P(A\cap C)-P(B\cap C)+P(A\cap B\cap C)
formulas.plain: P(A or B or C) = P(A)+P(B)+P(C) - P(AB) - P(AC) - P(BC) + P(ABC)
formulas.whenToUse: When computing the union of three (or more) events using inclusion-exclusion.

terms.means: probability all three events occur simultaneously

formulas.trap: Forgetting the triple intersection term (+P(A∩B∩C)) is a classic sign error.

formulas.name: Prior, Likelihood, Posterior
formulas.plain: Posterior is proportional to Likelihood times Prior
formulas.whenToUse: When performing Bayesian inference: updating belief about hypothesis H after seeing evidence E.

terms.means: likelihood of evidence E under H
posterior probability of H after E

formulas.trap: The proportionality sign means the denominator P(E) is a normalizing constant; it is the same for all hypotheses.

formulas.name: Sensitivity, Specificity, and Predictive Value
formulas.plain: Sensitivity = P(test positive | disease present); Specificity = P(test negative | no disease); PPV = P(disease | test positive)
formulas.whenToUse: When interpreting diagnostic test problems or binary classification problems in GATE questions.
formulas.trap: High sensitivity/specificity does NOT guarantee high PPV when the base rate (prior) is very low.
formulas.reference: Probability and Statistics / Bayes Theorem Applications

formulas.name: Conditional Probability on a Partition
formulas.plain: P(A|B) obtained either from the definition or directly from Bayes with two partitions
formulas.whenToUse: Connecting the definition form and Bayes form — useful when asked to compute posterior step by step.
formulas.trap: Students compute P(B) incorrectly by ignoring the complement branch P(B|A^c)*P(A^c).
formulas.reference: Probability and Statistics / Conditional Probability and Bayes
